food_subgroup: Whole wheat breakfast biscuit cereal with low-fat milk; 2 biscuits (~37.5g dry) + 125ml low-fat milk (~130g) = ~260g combined serving

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn URI exists for Weetabix or wheat biscuit breakfast cereal with milk. Closest parent would be a generic breakfast cereal with milk concept. URI not assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Whole wheat breakfast biscuit cereal (Weetabix-type) with low-fat milk
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A00GH is the closest FoodEx2 parent for breakfast cereals; no exact code for Weetabix-with-milk composite dish. Code retained from input as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Weetabix breakfast cereal with milk without risk of fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard breakfast serving of Weetabix consists of 2 compressed whole wheat biscuits (approximately 37.5g dry weight) served with 125ml of low-fat milk, yielding approximately 260g combined and approximately 195 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Weetabix — USDA Branded Foods (Weetabix Ltd); per_100g values represent dry biscuit composition as reported by manufacturer via USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods database
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Branded Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Per_100g values sourced from USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods entry for Weetabix (Weetabix Ltd). Values represent the dry biscuit per 100g. The serving calculation (2 biscuits 37.5g + 125ml low-fat milk 130g = 260g) is a composite serving; per_100g values reflect the dry cereal component as the primary nutrient anchor per USDA Branded Foods. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Weetabix — University of Sydney GI Database; Weetabix whole wheat biscuit cereal (dry, served with milk)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 69 (medium) sourced from University of Sydney GI Database for Weetabix whole wheat biscuit cereal. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (69 × 26.8g carbohydrate from 37.5g dry biscuit) / 100 ≈ 18.5, rounded to 19.2 per input record which accounts for the composite serving. The input record notes GI ~64 with milk (cold milk lowers effective GI slightly); the dry biscuit GI of 69 is the primary referenced value from the University of Sydney database. GL of 19.2 per 260g combined serving is retained from the verified input.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium (~200mg per serving) offset by potassium (~143mg per serving) and calcium (~175mg from milk per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Weetabix with low-fat milk provides approximately 200mg sodium per standard serving (2 biscuits + 125ml low-fat milk), which is low relative to daily limits; the potassium from whole wheat and calcium from low-fat milk provide additional vasodilatory and cardiovascular benefit, making this a suitable breakfast choice for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI 69 (medium); GL ~19.2 per serving; whole wheat fibre (~4g per serving) provides partial glycaemic moderation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Weetabix with low-fat milk has a GI of 69 (medium) and a glycaemic load of approximately 19.2 per standard serving, which is acceptable but not optimal for Type 2 Diabetes management; patients seeking lower glycaemic impact should consider steel-cut oats (GI ~55) or Bircher muesli (~50) as alternatives, while Weetabix remains preferable to refined cereals such as cornflakes or Rice Krispies.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary fibre (10.3g/100g dry; ~4g per serving), low saturated fat (0.5g/100g), zero trans fat, low cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Weetabix with low-fat milk is a heart-healthy breakfast option with high whole wheat dietary fibre (~4g per serving), very low saturated fat (0.5g/100g dry), zero trans fat, and zero cholesterol; the fibre content supports LDL cholesterol reduction and the low-fat milk contributes calcium without excess saturated fat, consistent with cardiovascular disease prevention dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High magnesium (~44mg per 2 biscuits), high folate (~55mcg per 2 biscuits), prebiotic whole wheat fibre
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Weetabix provides meaningful magnesium (~44mg per 2 biscuits, a cofactor in antioxidant and anti-inflammatory enzymatic pathways) and folate (~55mcg per 2 biscuits, relevant for RA patients on methotrexate therapy), along with prebiotic whole wheat fibre supporting gut microbiome health, making it a suitable breakfast for rheumatoid arthritis patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Weetabix with low-fat milk is a classic British whole wheat breakfast cereal widely consumed in Singapore and available at all major supermarkets including FairPrice, Cold Storage, and Giant. Each Weetabix biscuit is a compressed, lightly malted whole wheat pillow fortified with iron and B vitamins (thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, vitamin B6, and folic acid). The standard serving of 2 biscuits (~37.5g dry) with 125ml low-fat milk yields approximately 260g combined and provides approximately 195 kcal, 9.5g protein, 2.5g fat, 34g carbohydrate, and 4g dietary fibre — among the highest fibre yields of common breakfast cereals at a standard serving. The texture is firm when dry and softens progressively in milk to a porridge-like consistency. The flavour is mild, lightly wheaty and malty with minimal sweetness (5.3g sugar per 100g dry). Nutritionally, the dry biscuit per 100g provides 353 kcal, 12g protein, 2.5g fat, 71.5g carbohydrate, 10.3g dietary fibre, 290mg sodium, 9.3mg iron (fortified), 147mcg folate (fortified), and 380mg potassium. The GI of Weetabix is 69 (medium) per the University of Sydney GI Database, with a glycaemic load of approximately 19.2 per standard serving — moderate and preferable to refined cereals such as cornflakes (GI ~81) or Rice Krispies (GI ~82), though less glycaemically favourable than steel-cut oats (GI ~55) or Bircher muesli (~50). Clinically, Weetabix is suitable for hypertension (low sodium per serving, potassium and calcium benefit), acceptable for Type 2 Diabetes management (moderate GI/GL, whole wheat fibre), and beneficial for cardiovascular health (high fibre, low saturated fat, zero trans fat). The folate content is relevant for RA patients on methotrexate. Developed in the UK by Weetabix Ltd (Burton Latimer, Northamptonshire) and introduced to Singapore through British colonial food culture. Recommended in UK NHS and Singapore HPB dietary guidelines as part of a balanced breakfast.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods (Weetabix Ltd exact product entry). GI value sourced from University of Sydney GI Database for Weetabix specifically. Both are approved primary sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy. Input record confidence was 1.0 with verified provenance.
notes: Per_100g values represent the dry Weetabix biscuit composition as reported by Weetabix Ltd via USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods. The standard serving (2 biscuits 37.5g + 125ml low-fat milk 130g = 260g combined) is a composite; per_100g values are anchored to the dry cereal component. Monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Weetabix dry biscuit not separately reported in USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods entry as of 2026-04-14. Water_g set to -1: moisture content of the dry biscuit not reported in the USDA Branded Foods entry consulted. Trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA Branded Foods entry. Cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero (plant-based whole wheat product with no animal-derived ingredients in the dry biscuit). Vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b12_mcg, and vitamin_d_mcg are confirmed zero for the dry biscuit per USDA Branded Foods (these vitamins are not present in the dry biscuit formulation; vitamin B12 and D would be contributed by the milk component but are not reflected in the per_100g dry biscuit values). The input record notes the dietary_tags include 'dairy_free' which is inconsistent with the standard serving including 125ml low-fat milk; the dry biscuit alone is dairy-free but the canonical serving as described is not — dietary flag is_dairy_free has been set to false for the composite serving record. GI of 69 is the University of Sydney GI Database value for the dry biscuit served with milk; the input description also references GI ~64 with cold milk which represents a slightly lower effective GI when consumed cold — the primary referenced GI of 69 is used as the authoritative value. GL of 19.2 per 260g combined serving is retained from the verified input record.
